2023 · ICLR 2024 · Agents

MetaGPT: Meta Programming for a Multi-Agent Collaborative Framework

Turns a team of LLM agents into a simulated software company — Product Manager, Architect, Project Manager, Engineer, QA Engineer — coordinated by human Standardized Operating Procedures encoded as prompts, a shared structured message pool, and an executable-feedback loop, so complex tasks don't collapse into cascading hallucination.

Sirui Hong, Mingchen Zhuge, Jiaqi Chen, Xiawu Zheng, Yuheng Cheng, Ceyao Zhang, Jinlin Wang, Zili Wang, Steven Ka Shing Yau, Zijuan Lin, Liyang Zhou, Chenyu Ran, Lingfeng Xiao, Chenglin Wu, Jürgen Schmidhuber
venue ICLR 2024 citations 2,140 topic Agents source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-07-19
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By mid-2023, LLM-based multi-agent systems could hold a dialogue but fell apart on anything that required sustained, coherent work: chaining several agents together naively causes their hallucinations to cascade — one agent's confabulated detail becomes the next agent's ground truth, with nothing in between to catch it. Prior frameworks like CAMEL's role-play and ChatDev's chat chain leaned on unconstrained natural language as the sole interface between agents. The authors reach for the children's "telephone game" as the apt analogy: after enough rounds of free-form back-and-forth, the original intent is barely recognizable, and role-play framings tend to degenerate into unproductive chit-chat ("Hi, hello and how are you?" — "Great! Have you had lunch?") rather than functional handoffs.

What was missing was the thing that keeps human teams from making these same mistakes: Standardized Operating Procedures (SOPs). A real software company's Product Manager doesn't verbally improvise a set of requirements to an Engineer — she writes a structured Product Requirements Document that the whole team can inspect and validate. Existing LLM multi-agent work had neither the role specialization nor the structured, verifiable intermediate artifacts to reproduce that discipline, so errors that a human process would catch at a handoff instead propagated silently to the final output.

// how it works

The method

MetaGPT encodes human SOPs directly into the prompts and workflow of five specialized agent roles — Product Manager, Architect, Project Manager, Engineer, and QA Engineer — each initialized with its own profile (name, goal, constraints) and tool access (the Product Manager can use a web-search tool; the Engineer can execute code). Given a one-line human requirement, the roles run in a strict sequential pipeline (Figure 1): the Product Manager produces a Product Requirements Document (user stories plus a prioritized requirement pool); the Architect turns that into a system design — file lists, data structures, interface definitions, and a program call-flow diagram; the Project Manager decomposes the design into a task list; Engineers implement and run the code file by file; and the QA Engineer writes and runs unit tests as a code-quality gate.

Two mechanisms hold the pipeline together beyond just sequencing roles. First, structured communication: instead of dialogue, agents publish structured documents (PRDs, designs, task lists, code) to a shared message pool and subscribe only to the message types relevant to their role — the Architect mainly reads PRDs, the Engineer mainly reads designs and tasks — which sidesteps both the "telephone game" distortion of relayed natural language and the coordination overhead of a fully pairwise messaging topology. Second, executable feedback: after the Engineer writes code, it executes the code together with its own generated unit tests; on failure, the Engineer consults the PRD, design, and code history stored in memory, debugs, and retries — up to 3 times — before handing off, giving the pipeline a genuine self-correction loop that pure code-review approaches lack.

// what they found

The results

On the standard static code benchmarks, MetaGPT reports Pass@1 of 85.9% on HumanEval (164 tasks) and 87.7% on MBPP (427 tasks) — both stated as new state-of-the-art, ahead of the paper's own GPT-4 baseline (67.0%, no framework) and the strongest prior code-generation baseline it cites, Codex+CodeT (65.8%/67.7%). Turning off the executable-feedback mechanism drops these to 81.7%/82.3%, a 4.2/5.4-point contribution the authors attribute to runtime self-correction.

The more interesting comparison is SoftwareDev, a 70-task, self-authored benchmark of realistic end-to-end software projects (mini-games, data-visualization tools, CRUD apps); the main paper reports on 7 representative tasks graded head-to-head against AutoGPT, LangChain+REPL, AgentVerse, and ChatDev, with MetaGPT itself running on GPT-4 (the paper states GPT-4 32K specifically for its separate 11-task appendix run). MetaGPT scores 3.75/4 on a human-rated Executability scale (1 = non-functional, 4 = flawless) versus ChatDev's 2.25 (Table 1). A separate appendix table (Table 4) breaks the same style of comparison down per task and adds AutoGPT, LangChain, and AgentVerse: all three score a flat 1.0 on every one of the 7 tasks — not a single runnable program — while Table 4's own per-task average comes out to MetaGPT 3.9 / ChatDev 2.1, close to but not identical to Table 1's 3.75/2.25 for what is nominally the same comparison. It finishes in 541s (with feedback) versus ChatDev's 762s, and needs 0.83 average human corrections versus ChatDev's 2.5 — though it burns more total tokens per task (31,255 vs. ChatDev's 19,292) even as its per-code-line "productivity" is better (124.3 vs. 248.9 tokens/line). A small role ablation (2 tasks; Table 3) shows Executability climbing from 1.0 (Engineer-only, 10 average human revisions) to 4.0 (all four roles, 2.5 revisions) as roles are added one at a time.

SoftwareDev (7-task subset)ChatDevMetaGPT (w/o feedback)MetaGPT
Executability (1–4)2.253.673.75

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The headline "85.9% vs. GPT-4's 67.0%" framing on HumanEval is more fragile than it looks, and — unusually — the paper's own appendix (Table 7) proves it. In a follow-up experiment, the authors call bare gpt-4-0613 directly on HumanEval under three settings: (A) a plain API call scores 72.4% average Pass@1 over five runs; (B) adding regex parsing of the response pushes that to 81.2%; (C) adding a system prompt plus parsing gets 80.0%. In other words, most of the 19-point gap the abstract implies between "GPT-4" (67.0%) and MetaGPT (85.9%) is closeable with prompt and post-processing engineering alone, before a single multi-agent role gets involved — the 67.0% figure is OpenAI's own zero-shot number from a different paper, not a matched-methodology control run by these authors. The multi-agent pipeline's own contribution, isolated cleanly, is closer to the feedback-mechanism ablation the paper itself reports: 81.7% → 85.9%, i.e. roughly 4 points.

The SoftwareDev comparison — the paper's more qualitative, "does this actually build working software" evidence — is thin in ways typical of this genre of demo benchmark, and doesn't fully agree with itself. The main-body Table 1 reports Executability of 2.25 (ChatDev) and 3.75 (MetaGPT); the appendix's Table 4, covering what reads as the same 7-task ChatDev-vs-MetaGPT comparison plus three additional baselines, computes its own per-task average at 2.1 (ChatDev) and 3.9 (MetaGPT) — close, but not the same number, for what the paper presents as one comparison. Neither table states whether its 7 tasks are identical to the other's, so a reader can't tell if this is rounding, a slightly different task subset, or a transcription slip — but it means the paper's own headline Executability figures aren't internally reproducible from the numbers it publishes. Executability itself is graded by the authors on a coarse 1–4 scale with no stated inter-rater agreement, blinding protocol, or rubric beyond a one-line description per score, and the main-paper table covers only 7 of the dataset's 70 tasks (the appendix's 11-task, 70-task-average numbers are reported only for MetaGPT itself, not for any baseline, so they can't be cross-checked either). The baselines are also suspicious in their uniformity: AutoGPT, LangChain, and AgentVerse score a flat 1.0 — total failure — on every single task in Table 4, with the paper attributing this to short, logic-poor, cross-file-dependency-broken code, but not stating what LLM backend or configuration effort went into running those baselines versus MetaGPT's own GPT-4 backend. A framework this uniformly at zero raises the question of whether it was tuned at all, and the comparison would be far more convincing with matched backbones and at least an order of magnitude more tasks.

baselines

GPT-4's 67.0% comes from a different paper's zero-shot number, not a re-run control; the paper's own appendix shows a matched-methodology GPT-4 baseline reaches 72.4–81.2% with only prompt/parsing changes. The SoftwareDev baselines (AutoGPT/LangChain/AgentVerse) all score a flat 1.0 with no stated backbone or tuning effort disclosed.

contamination

Not discussed. HumanEval and MBPP are two of the most widely circulated code benchmarks; the paper doesn't address whether GPT-4's pretraining could have seen these exact problems, despite the multi-agent framework's advantage over a bare-GPT-4 control being modest once post-processing is controlled for.

what's missed

The introduction's claim that MetaGPT "achieves a 100% task completion rate" doesn't map cleanly onto any reported table — the closest metrics are the 1–4 Executability scores (3.75/3.9) and the binary !/% capability table, neither of which is a completion rate. Cost and token comparisons against baselines aren't normalized for backbone model, undermining the "efficiency" framing.

would raise confidence

Re-run GPT-4 with matched prompting/parsing as the explicit control (not a cited external number); grade Executability blind with multiple raters and report agreement; run SoftwareDev across all 70 tasks, not 7; state the backbone and tuning budget given to each baseline framework.

// bottom line

The engineering pattern here — SOPs as prompts, role specialization with structured deliverables, a shared pub/sub message pool, and an executable-feedback retry loop — is a genuinely influential recipe that shaped a wave of later role-based agent frameworks. But the paper's own numbers undercut its headline framing: the GPT-4-vs-MetaGPT HumanEval gap shrinks by roughly three-quarters once post-processing is matched, and the SoftwareDev win, while plausible, rests on 7 tasks, unblinded human grading, and baselines that mysteriously fail every single time.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Role specialization with concrete, structured deliverables (a PRD, a design doc, a task list, code, tests) — rather than open-ended chat between agents — is the core recipe for keeping multi-agent hallucination from cascading.
  2. A shared message pool with publish/subscribe communication beats pairwise dialogue: each role reads only what's relevant to it, avoiding both "telephone game" distortion and full-mesh chatter overhead.
  3. Executable feedback — run the code, feed the error back, retry a bounded number of times — is a cheap, high-leverage addition; MetaGPT's own ablation credits it with 4.2–5.4 points of Pass@1, and it's now table stakes for coding agents.
  4. Read the appendix before the abstract: MetaGPT's own Table 7 shows a bare, well-prompted GPT-4 closes most of the paper's headline 85.9%-vs-67.0% gap — a reminder that "framework beats base model" comparisons need a matched-methodology control, not a cited number from elsewhere.
